title: Learning with Expected Signatures: Theory and Applications

reviews.summary: The paper studies the statistical estimation of the expected signature of continuous-time stochastic processes from discretely observed paths. It provides general in-fill and long-span asymptotic results—consistency and a central limit theorem—for empirical estimators of expected signature coordinates under broad regularity and dependence assumptions, and introduces a simple control-variate “martingale correction” that substantially reduces estimator variance when (some components of) the latent process is a martingale. Theoretical results are complemented by examples (BM, fBm, CAR/OU, Heston) and experiments demonstrating practical gains in several signature-based learning tasks.
strengths: Provides unified L^m in-fill convergence (Theorem 2.8) for discrete expected signature estimators to their continuous-time counterparts under flexible continuity/conditional-moment conditions (Aα–Aδ), covering non-semimartingale settings (e.g., fBm with H>1/2) and irregular, sample-dependent partitions.

Establishes a long-span CLT (Theorem 2.10) under strong mixing with a clean separation of discretization and statistical error via conditions (11)–(13), and a feasible HAC-type variance estimator (Corollary 2.12).

Introduces a control-variate estimator tailored to signature coordinates that leverages martingale structure for variance reduction, with a practical estimator for the optimal coefficient c and a generalization to partial martingales.

Demonstrates variance reduction and accuracy improvements in synthetic studies for option pricing under BM/Heston and in learning tasks (classification via GPES and distributional regression via SES).
Verifies assumptions on representative processes (BM, fBm, CAR/OU, Heston) and clarifies when mixing or covariance-decay conditions apply.
Careful set-up of canonical geometric processes and signature-defining partitions, with an accessible glossary and clear decomposition of estimation error.
Good balance between main-text intuition and technical appendices (sewing-type lemmas, mixing-to-ergodicity implications, Gaussian covariance decay).

Bridges a key gap between continuous-time expected signature theory and computable discrete estimators that underpin many signature-based ML methods, offering a probabilistic interpretation and finite-sample improvements that can impact practice.
Complementary to analytical/PDE/cumulant approaches in prior work by addressing the model-free, data-driven estimation regime prevalent in ML pipelines.
weaknesses: Strong mixing is a relatively stringent assumption for the CLT; the paper gives a Gaussian alternative (Theorem 2.14) for consistency but not a CLT under weaker dependence (e.g., long memory), and does not treat non-mixing regimes more generally.

The martingale correction is analyzed for martingale inputs; when used as a “data transform” in non-martingale settings it introduces bias, but there is no theoretical MSE trade-off or guidance on selecting/regularizing c to control bias.
Verification of conditions (Aβ) and (Aγ) may be nontrivial outside the presented examples; more readily checkable sufficient conditions would increase applicability.

Experiments are synthetic only; no real-world datasets are reported. Some large accuracy gains (e.g., OU in GPES) lack a deeper diagnostic (bias/variance decomposition, sensitivity to partition mesh, truncation level).
The impact of estimating c (versus oracle cπ*) on asymptotics is not analyzed; finite-sample regression-based estimation can introduce additional variability and potential overfitting.
No direct comparison to analytical expected-signature computations (e.g., recent cumulant-based recursions) where available, to validate estimator bias at small mesh.
A few minor typos/notation inconsistencies (e.g., hats vs. Π(N) superscripts; stray “option Checkbox”) and occasional dense passages around Assumption 2.6 and Lemma usage.
The link between truncation level, p-variation order, and practical feature computation could be stated more explicitly for practitioners.

While analytical approaches (PDEs, cumulants/expected signature of semimartingales) are cited, a more explicit positioning versus log-signature/cumulant recursions (e.g., Friz–Hager–Tapia 2024) in terms of numerical estimation could help.
Recent signature-based finite-sample/block-averaging perspectives and non-asymptotic bounds are not discussed; adding a brief perspective would contextualize the asymptotic focus.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide theoretical MSE analyses for the martingale correction when the process is not a martingale (e.g., a bias–variance decomposition with bounds in terms of deviations from martingality), or at least a diagnostic to decide when to enable it?
2. How sensitive are your asymptotic conclusions to estimating c with the regression slope? Does √N-consistency of ĉ hold under your assumptions, and does plugging in ĉ alter the CLT for the corrected estimator?
3. Can you offer more user-friendly sufficient conditions that imply (Aβ) and (Aγ), beyond the example classes in the appendix—e.g., conditions on Markov generators or covariance kernels that practitioners can check?
4. In the pricing experiments, could you report empirical variance ratios and correlations between S^I and S_c^I to verify the predicted 1−ρ^2 variance reduction?
5. How does truncation level (and the choice of words I) affect the rates and constants in practice? Any guidance for balancing truncation error, mesh size, and sample size?
6. Could you include at least one real-data experiment (e.g., financial time series classification/regression) to illustrate robustness beyond synthetic settings?
7. For Theorem 2.14, is there a path to a CLT under covariance-decay conditions (without strong mixing), perhaps in the Gaussian case using Breuer–Major or martingale approximations tailored to signature functionals?
reviews.assessment: This paper makes a solid and timely contribution by giving a general asymptotic theory (consistency and CLT) for expected signature estimation from discrete observations, under assumptions that cover many processes of interest and realistic observation schemes. The martingale control variate is simple yet effective and, together with the asymptotics, strengthens the probabilistic footing of several signature-based ML methods. While some conditions (e.g., strong mixing) are demanding and parts of the analysis (e.g., bias control for the martingale correction in non-martingale settings) remain largely empirical, the theoretical developments are sound, the examples are representative, and the empirical section—though limited to synthetic data—demonstrates practical value. I recommend acceptance after minor revisions focused on clarifying assumption verification, analyzing the plug-in c estimator’s effect, and deepening experimental diagnostics; these would further increase the work’s impact and usability for the ICML community.
